abstract: This Nature editorial argues that scientists who study societal division have a responsibility to apply their expertise toward reducing polarization. It highlights emerging research on how framing local policies in personal or 'proximate' terms can soften ideological responses, and calls for more rigorous study and testing of interventions. The piece situates this work within a broader concern about rising global conflict, political violence, and threats to scientific collaboration.
key_claims: Societies are becoming increasingly polarized, with measurable behavioral consequences including reduced inter-group interaction and more negative perceptions across identity lines.
Researchers in political science, sociology, psychology, and history have both the tools and the responsibility to develop and test strategies for reducing polarization.
Focusing on shared concerns and proximate, personal framings of policies can blur ideological divides.
Research on depolarization interventions is still in its infancy and requires more rigorous, cross-context evaluation.
Science-for-diplomacy and open international collaboration are valuable counterweights to current trends toward militarization and political interference in research.
methods: Editorial commentary synthesizing recent published research rather than presenting original empirical work.
Drawing on Lucas et al.'s survey of 4,000 self-identified liberal and conservative Canadians comparing responses to generic versus personally framed local policy questions.
Citing Čehajić-Clancy and Halperin's 2024 Nature Reviews Psychology review of conflict and depolarization research.
Referencing Zhelnina's 2022 ethnographic-style study of Moscow residents organizing against Soviet-era building demolitions.
findings: In the Lucas et al. survey, respondents answered generically framed policy questions along ideological lines, but proximate framings produced less predictable, more ideologically mixed responses.
Examples include liberals not opposing local security cameras and conservatives supporting immigrant services in their municipality.
Zhelnina found that politically opposed Moscow residents set aside ideological differences to jointly campaign against demolition of their housing.
Stated intentions in surveys may diverge from actual behavior, and interventions effective in one context may not generalize.
contributions: Calls on the research community to take an active role in countering polarization rather than only studying it.
Highlights proximate policy framing as a promising mechanism for bridging ideological divides.
Connects depolarization research to broader debates about research funding, militarization, and the role of European science in global diplomacy.
Provides an accessible synthesis pointing readers toward recent key studies on conflict reduction and urban politics.
framing: The editorial positions itself within an emerging interdisciplinary conversation across political science, social psychology, and sociology on the causes of polarization and the design of interventions to reduce it. It frames scientists as having a civic obligation, aligning with calls in Nature Reviews Psychology and related literature for applied conflict research and science-for-diplomacy.
